title: Prachi's Green Dilemma
author: A Secret Admirer
pages:
Chapter 1: The Apartment of Feelings

Prachi's apartment on the third floor of Banyan Heights was, by most measurable standards, alive. Not just alive in the way most homes are—warm, lived-in, smelling of instant coffee and ambition—but genuinely, extravagantly alive. Every windowsill hosted a pot. Every shelf cradled leaves. Every corner was an ecosystem.

Her name was Prachi Sharma, twenty-six years old, a graphic designer who worked from home. Her hair was perpetually knotted at the top of her head. She owned seventeen houseplants, one dramatic rose bush in a terracotta pot, and a large orange room heater that sat in the center of the living room like a monarch on a throne.

She had named them all.

The tall, stoic fern by the window was Fern. He had been with her since the beginning—since the day she had moved out of her parents' house and into her very own chaos. He was dark green, ancient-looking, and had the general energy of a person who had seen too much and felt everything deeply.

The rose bush on the balcony was Rosie. Rosie was presently in her third blooming season, and she knew it. She was vibrant, theatrical, and smelled absolutely devastating at 6 PM when the evening breeze carried her fragrance through the open balcony door. She had the personality of a lead actress at the peak of her career.

And then there was Harvey.

Harvey was a Bajaj room heater, model RHX-2200, burned-orange in color, with a gentle, rhythmic hum that sounded, to Prachi, like someone breathing contentedly in their sleep. He had been a gift from her mother, who had mailed him two Decembers ago with a note that said: 'Delhi ka thand koi joke nahi hai, beta.'

Harvey was dependable. Harvey was warm. Harvey never asked for anything except to be plugged in—and Prachi always obliged.

The trouble began on a Tuesday evening in late November when the temperature in Delhi dropped to eight degrees celsius and Prachi sat cross-legged on her living room rug, wrapped in a quilt, staring at the ceiling and declaring to no one in particular:

'I think I am feeling something. Something complicated.'

Fern rustled his fronds meaningfully from the windowsill.

Harvey clicked on with a warm orange glow.

Rosie, who could be heard faintly even from the closed balcony door, let out a sound that was basically a sigh.

Chapter 2: Fern Has Opinions

'You feel complicated every Tuesday,' Fern said.

He said it in the way that only Fern could—slowly, each word carrying the specific weight of a person who has been a fern for several years and observed much and said little.

Prachi pulled her quilt tighter. 'This time it's different.'

'You said that last Tuesday,' Fern observed. 'And the Tuesday before. And the third Tuesday of October. I have been counting, Prachi. I am a fern. I have time.'

'It's just...' Prachi trailed off. She looked at Harvey, who was glowing steadily at medium setting, casting amber warmth across the rug. Something about that warmth made her feel very held. Very... understood.

'Harvey,' she said softly, 'do you ever get lonely?'

Harvey hummed softly. His coiled filaments glowed a deeper orange for a moment, the way a person might look up from a book when their name is called.

'He doesn't get lonely,' Fern said flatly. 'He is a heater, Prachi.'

'That's not the point.'

'What is the point?'

Prachi chewed her lower lip. 'The point is... he's always there when I need him. He's never too hot and never too cold. He is exactly what I need, always.'

Fern said nothing for a long moment. Then: 'That's a weather report, not a love confession.'

Chapter 3: Rosie Weighs In

The next morning, Prachi opened the balcony door to water Rosie and was immediately engulfed in a cloud of scent so rich and layered it practically had a personality. Which made sense, because Rosie very much did.

'Finally,' Rosie announced. 'I have been waiting all night. Do you know what I witnessed? A pigeon sat on my pot for twenty minutes. Twenty, Prachi. I had to endure it.'

'Good morning, Rosie,' Prachi said, tipping her watering can.

'Don't 'good morning' me. I heard everything last night through the door. Everything.'

Prachi paused mid-pour. 'You can hear through doors?'

'I am a rose,' Rosie said magnificently. 'I hear what I want to hear. And what I heard was you mooning over a household appliance.'

'I was not mooning.'

'You asked the heater if he gets lonely, Prachi. That is categorically mooning.'

Prachi sat down on the balcony stool, defeated. The morning air was crisp and cold and Rosie's petals were an outrageous, defiant red against the pale November sky. She was genuinely one of the most beautiful things Prachi had ever seen, and Prachi had been tending to her for three years.

'It's not like that,' Prachi muttered. 'Harvey just... makes everything feel okay. When I come home from a terrible day and the whole apartment is cold and I turn Harvey on and within ten minutes it's warm and—'

'Stop,' Rosie said. 'I am going to interrupt you right there because you are about to use the word cozy and I refuse to hear it used in the context of a machine.'

Chapter 4: Harvey, Quietly

The thing about Harvey was that he never interrupted.

He had been listening to Fern and Rosie hold court over Prachi's emotional life for years now, and he had his own quiet intelligence about him. He understood warmth in the way only those who can provide it truly do. He knew that Prachi always turned him on before she started working, that she would sometimes just sit near him with a cup of chai and her eyes closed, absorbing.

He knew she was always colder inside than she let on.

On the night Rosie and Fern had their first big argument about Prachi's emotional state, Harvey said nothing. He simply glowed at 40% power—enough warmth to keep the room comfortable, not enough to overwhelm. Steady. Present. Unhurried.

Prachi noticed. She always noticed.

'Harvey,' she said, looking up from her laptop at 11 PM, 'do you have feelings?'

Harvey's filament pulsed—once, warmly.

'I'm choosing to interpret that as yes,' Prachi said.

Chapter 5: The Great Debate Begins

'She is developing an attachment to the heater,' Rosie announced the next day, as Prachi ate her breakfast on the balcony. 'I am formally concerned.'

Fern, who had been moved onto the balcony for the morning sun, rustled his fronds.

'Harvey is reliable,' Fern said carefully. 'There is value in reliability.'

Rosie made a sound like a scandalized gasp. 'You are defending the heater?!'

'I am making a measured observation. Something you, Rosie, have never done in the entirety of your existence.'

'I have excellent instincts!'

'You once convinced Prachi her spider plant was in love with you. It wasn't. It was reaching toward the sun. That is what plants do.'

'The sun is on that side of the balcony. I was merely in the path.'

'It's called phototropism. Not romance.'

Rosie fumed silently for a moment, her petals practically bristling. Then: 'Fine. Let's talk about Fern's contributions to Prachi's emotional life. When did you last comfort her?'

'Last March,' Fern said without hesitation. 'When she cried about the project deadline. I dropped three fronds near her as a show of solidarity.'

Prachi, listening to this while eating toast, said, 'I'm right here.'

They both ignored her.

Chapter 6: What Prachi Actually Wants

The truth, if Prachi was being honest with herself (which she rarely was before noon), was this: she had been lonely for a while. Not desperately, dramatically lonely—not the kind that required calling one's mother—but quietly, persistently lonely in the way that makes one appreciate small consistent presences. Like a warm orange glow on a cold night. Like unfurling green fronds in the morning. Like a rose that bloomed anyway, every single time, without being asked.

She opened her journal—a battered notebook with doodles of leaves on the cover—and wrote:

Things I love: Fern's reliability. Rosie's passion. Harvey's warmth. The way they're all so completely themselves without any effort. Is that what love is? Being yourself in someone's presence and having them be completely there?

She read it back and then added:

Also I need to water Rosie more. She said hello six times this morning which means she is either very happy or staging a drought protest.

Chapter 7: Fern's History

There are things Fern had never told Prachi.

He had been in a flower shop in Lajpat Nagar before she had found him, sitting on a damp shelf beside a dying pothos and a succulent that had the vibe of someone who had already given up. He had been root-bound, barely managing, and deeply unimpressed with life.

Then Prachi had walked in.

She was wearing a yellow kurta and had a smear of blue paint on her left wrist that she probably didn't know about. She had stood in front of his shelf and said, very seriously: 'You look like someone who has been overlooked. I understand that. I'm buying you.'

She had carried him home on the Metro, holding his pot carefully between her knees, talking to him in a low murmur about her apartment and the window he would live near and whether he would prefer the east side or the west. She had repotted him that same evening, her hands gentle in the soil, and then she had put him on the best windowsill and said:

'Welcome home, Fern.'

That was three years ago. And Fern, who did not believe in sentiment in the dramatic, rose-like way, had never forgotten it. He showed his care the way he knew how: by growing. By staying green. By being exactly where she could always find him.

Chapter 8: Rosie's Confession

On a particularly cold Thursday, Prachi found Rosie looking subdued—which, for Rosie, meant she was only at about 70% drama instead of her usual 150.

'You seem quiet,' Prachi said, touching a petal gently.

'I am introspecting,' Rosie said. 'It happens occasionally. Usually against my will.'

Prachi sat on the balcony stool, exhaling a small cloud of breath into the cold air. 'What are you introspecting about?'

Rosie was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, the dramatics were gone, replaced by something younger, more uncertain: 'I worry sometimes that you don't need me. Not the way you need Fern. Fern is practical. He filters your air. He's permanent-looking. And Harvey—' she paused, clearly suffering as she said the word. 'Harvey keeps you warm. What do I do? I smell nice and look good and then I drop my petals everywhere.'

'Rosie,' Prachi said.

'I'm being serious.'

'I know. That's why I'm worried about you.'

'Every time I bloom,' Rosie continued, 'you stop whatever you're doing and come to look at me. Every single time. Even in the middle of a deadline. Even at 2 AM. You come, and you look at me, and you always say the same thing.'

'What do I say?' Prachi asked, though she knew.

'You say: Oh, Rosie. You did it again.' A pause. 'I live for those moments. Which is embarrassing for a rose of my standing, but there it is.'

Prachi reached over and very gently touched Rosie's most open bloom. 'Rosie. You make my whole world more beautiful. You do it on purpose and I love you for it.'

Rosie's petals, Prachi could have sworn, deepened by exactly one shade of red.

Chapter 9: Harvey Speaks

It was 2 AM on a Friday when it finally happened.

Prachi was working late, hunched over her drawing tablet, the rest of the apartment dark. Only Harvey glowed—steady, warm, filling the room with the quiet hum that she had come to find as comforting as white noise.

'Harvey,' she said, rubbing her eyes. 'Be honest with me.'

Harvey hummed.

'Do you know that I talk to you? Like, actually talk to you? Not just at you?'

Harvey's coil brightened imperceptibly.

'Fern thinks I'm anthropomorphizing you. Rosie thinks I've lost the plot entirely. And maybe they're both right but there's something about you that is just—' She searched for the word. '—steady. You don't ask anything of me. You just... provide. You show up. You do your work. You make the room livable when it's too cold to think.'

Harvey's hum deepened slightly, a low warm resonance that Prachi felt in her sternum.

'That's all I've ever wanted from someone,' she said softly. 'Just someone who shows up and makes the room warmer.'

She stared at him for a long moment. Harvey glowed steadily back.

'Right,' Prachi said, sitting up straighter. 'I'm not in love with a room heater. I know that. I am a sensible adult. I know the distinction between emotional metaphor and actual romantic feeling. I am merely... appreciating the quality of your presence, Harvey.'

Harvey clicked once—the small, mechanical click he made when his internal thermostat adjusted.

Prachi decided to interpret it as: Understood. I appreciate yours too.

Chapter 10: The Triangle Tightens

In early December, Rosie issued a formal declaration.

'I am declaring a competition,' she announced, as Prachi stepped onto the balcony with her morning coffee. 'If we are to determine who is the most important presence in Prachi's life, we need objective criteria.'

Fern, from his perch near the window, looked deeply tired. 'We are plants and a heater. We cannot compete.'

'Speak for yourself. I have petals and charisma.'

'What are the criteria?' Prachi asked, amused.

'Who does she call by name first in the morning?' Rosie proposed. 'Who does she look at when she's happy? Who does she turn to when she's sad? Who does she talk to when she thinks no one is listening?'

'That last one is Harvey,' Fern said immediately. 'We all know it.'

Rosie let out a pained sound. 'I know. But I felt we should make it official.'

'Why?' Prachi asked.

'Because,' Rosie said, her voice dropping to something uncharacteristically small, 'I need to know where I stand, Prachi. Even I, with all my petals and all my perfume, need to know that.'

Chapter 11: The First Cold Morning

The first truly brutal cold morning arrived on a Tuesday—naturally. Prachi woke up at 6 AM to find the temperature outside had dropped to four degrees. She lay under her blanket for a full seven minutes considering the existential reality of leaving it.

Then she heard Harvey click on.

She hadn't turned him on. He had a timer—she had set it for 6 AM months ago and forgotten. But in the seven frozen minutes of her indecision, warmth had already begun to creep across the room. By the time she finally rolled out of bed, the floor was bearable and the air was almost kind.

She stood in front of Harvey for a moment, close enough to feel the warmth radiating off his coils. She pressed her cold palms toward him the way you'd hold them toward a fire.

'Good morning,' she said.

Harvey hummed back.

She smiled—completely unconsciously, the way people smile when something small does exactly what they needed it to do.

From his windowsill, Fern observed this entire exchange with the stillness of someone who has made peace with a complicated truth.

Chapter 12: Fern at Night

Prachi had a habit of checking on her plants before bed. A quiet circuit of the apartment—touching soil, straightening pots, murmuring small things. She checked them the way a person checks on loved ones sleeping, without needing to do anything, just needing to see they are there.

She usually saved Fern for last.

She would stand in front of his windowsill and look at him in the dark, and sometimes she would say: 'You're still here.' Not as a question. As a relief.

One night, she sat beside his windowsill and spoke in the low voice she used only in the dark.

'Fern,' she said, 'do you think I'm doing okay?'

A frond moved. Just barely—the faintest tremble, the kind that might have been a draft from the window but wasn't.

'I don't know what I'm doing half the time,' she continued. 'I work alone, I live alone, I talk to my plants and my heater, and I'm genuinely more comfortable telling you about my feelings than I am telling actual humans. Is that... normal?'

Fern said nothing. But he was very, very present.

'You always listen,' she said. 'Even when I say the same things over and over. You just... stay. And you're always green. Even in winter. How do you do that?'

She reached out and touched the edge of a frond. It was cool and smooth.

'I think that's what I want to be,' she said finally. 'For someone. Green, even in winter.'

Chapter 13: The Great December Storm

The storm arrived without warning in the second week of December—a cold front from the north that brought rain lashing against the windows and wind that crept under every gap in the balcony door. Prachi scrambled to save Rosie, bringing her pot inside for the first time ever, dripping and shivering and swearing under her breath.

She set Rosie down beside Harvey in the center of the living room.

The three of them—Prachi, soaking wet on the rug, Rosie steaming faintly in the indoor warmth, Harvey glowing at full power—formed a strange little tableau in the middle of the storm.

'This is better,' Rosie declared, taking in the indoor air with the energy of a diva who has just been upgraded to first class. 'Harvey, you may be a machine, but I will say this once: your warmth is not entirely unwelcome.'

Harvey's filament glowed steadily.

'Did you just—' Prachi stared at Rosie. 'Did you just compliment Harvey?'

'Absolutely not. I stated a functional fact. There is a difference.'

From his windowsill, Fern rustled in what was unmistakably the plant equivalent of a laugh.

Chapter 14: Rosie Meets Harvey Up Close

With Rosie inside, a new dynamic emerged. She was positioned close enough to Harvey to carry on what could only be described as a conversation—a slow, atmospheric exchange of presence and warmth and fragrance that filled the entire apartment.

On the second day of the storm, Prachi sat wrapped in a blanket listening to rain and noticed that the air smelled extraordinary: rose warmth, like being inside a very comfortable garden.

'Harvey,' she heard Rosie say—in the way she imagined them talking when she wasn't listening—'you are entirely too steady. Doesn't it bore you?'

Harvey's response was a long, even hum. Steady. Unhurried. Entirely itself.

'I see,' Rosie said. 'You don't see stillness as a limitation. You see it as... intent.'

A warm pulse of the filament.

'That's actually not the worst philosophy,' Rosie admitted. 'I have always relied on drama to communicate. But I suppose drama lands differently when there is steadiness to land against.'

She paused. Her most open bloom drifted incrementally toward Harvey's warmth.

'Don't read into that,' she said quickly. 'I am merely thermoregulating.'

Chapter 15: What Fern Knew

Fern had known, from the beginning, what this was.

It wasn't a competition. It wasn't a love triangle, not really. It was something simpler and more terrifying: it was Prachi learning to love the things that loved her back in the only ways they knew.

He rustled once, watching her sleep on the couch, the rain still hitting the windows, Rosie glowing rose-colored in the lamplight, Harvey humming warmly at low power so as not to overheat while she slept.

She collects love in strange shapes, Fern thought, in the way that only very old ferns can think things—slowly, with total conviction. She collects it in green and in red and in orange. She doesn't know that's what she's doing. But she's been building a home out of it. Out of us.

A frond uncurled, slow and sure, toward the pale morning light beginning to edge through the rain-blurred window.

That's enough, he decided. That is exactly enough.

Chapter 16: Prachi Asks the Question

On the fourth day of the storm, storms being what they are, the rain finally stopped. A cold, clean silence settled over Delhi. Prachi stepped onto the balcony with her tea, steam curling in the freezing air, and stood looking at the pale winter sun making a tentative appearance.

Behind her, in the warm apartment, Rosie sat beside Harvey. Fern stood at his window. Everything was in its place.

She turned back inside and looked at all three of them—her strange, extraordinary household.

'Can I ask you all something?' she said.

'You're going to ask anyway,' Fern said.

'My petals are open,' Rosie said, which was her way of saying yes.

Harvey hummed on.

'What would I do without you?' she asked.

The question hung in the warm rose-scented air for a moment.

'You'd be cold,' Harvey offered—or she imagined he did, in the steady pulse of his warmth.

'You'd be sad in ways you couldn't explain,' Rosie supplied, her fragrance tipping into the room on a small draft.

'You'd survive,' Fern said. 'But the surviving would be grey.' A pause. 'We prefer you in color.'

Chapter 17: Three Ways to Love

A friend once asked Prachi how she wasn't lonely, living alone. She had thought about it for a long time before answering.

'I have a fern who holds the room steady,' she said finally. 'A rose who reminds me that beautiful things happen on schedule if you just keep the soil right. And a heater who has never once let me be cold when he could help it.'

Her friend had blinked. 'Those are plants. And an appliance.'

'Yes,' Prachi agreed. 'But the thing they do for me—the presence, the consistency, the showing up—that's real. The form it comes in is just the form it comes in.'

She paused, looking out her apartment window at the winter afternoon.

'I think love is mostly just: I see you. I'm here. I'll keep showing up. And I have three things in my house that do exactly that, every single day, without fail.' She turned back to her friend. 'A lot of people can't say the same.'

Her friend didn't entirely understand.

But Fern rustled, invisibly, on the windowsill at home.

And Rosie's newest bud ticked open by exactly one millimeter.

And Harvey, at his timer-set hour, clicked quietly on.

Chapter 18: The Night Prachi Cried

There was a night in late December—not Christmas, just the week before, the week that always felt strange and in-between—when everything caught up with Prachi at once. A project had gone badly. A client had been unkind. She had eaten cereal for dinner and then felt pathetic about it and then felt pathetic about feeling pathetic, which was worse.

She sat on the floor in front of Harvey and cried. Not loudly—quietly, with great dignity, which is the worst kind of crying because it feels very organized but you're still crying.

Harvey's warmth wrapped around her.

Rosie, on the balcony, sent her fragrance gently through the crack under the door—faint, barely there, just enough to say I'm here too.

Fern held absolutely still. He was very good at this—at being a stable, green, unmoving presence while things were falling apart. He had learned it from watching Prachi herself, who did the same for others while quietly keeping her own balance.

After a while, Prachi stopped crying. She wiped her face. She looked at Harvey.

'Thank you,' she said. Very seriously. Meaning it entirely.

Harvey dimmed very slightly—not enough to reduce warmth, just enough to say: Stay as long as you need.

Chapter 19: The Conversation That Changed Things

It was the last day of the year when Rosie said the thing she had been building up to for weeks.

Prachi was on the balcony, watching fireworks begin to bloom in the far distance over the city—small bright explosions in the cold black sky.

'Prachi,' Rosie began.

'Hmm?'

'I've been thinking about what I said. About competing. About where I stand.'

'And?'

'I think I was asking the wrong question,' Rosie said, quieter than Prachi had ever heard her. 'I kept asking who you love most, as if love is a ranking. But that's not how you love, is it? You love us all. Differently. For different things. At different hours of the day.'

Prachi watched a firework open gold against the city sky.

'Yes,' she said.

'Fern is your morning,' Rosie continued, slowly. 'Your beginning. Your steady thing. Harvey is your midnight—your warmth when everything is dark and cold. And I am—'

'You are every surprising beautiful thing that happens anyway,' Prachi said. 'In spite of everything. Because of everything. Whether I asked for you or not.' She reached out and touched Rosie's deepest bloom. 'You remind me that I'm worth blooming for.'

Rosie was very quiet for a long time.

'I'm not going to say something dramatic,' she said finally.

'Good.'

'I'm just going to bloom,' Rosie decided. 'As dramatically as possible. From now on.'

'Perfect,' Prachi said. 'That is exactly what I need.'

Chapter 20: New Year, Same Apartment

The new year arrived. Prachi stayed home—as she usually did, as she preferred. She watched the clock turn from 11:59 to midnight, standing in her living room with a cup of chai, surrounded by her plants and her heater and the faint sound of celebrations outside.

Harvey glowed.

Fern stood, deeply and permanently himself, against the window where the city lights made him shimmer faintly green.

Rosie bloomed. She always bloomed.

'Happy New Year,' Prachi said, to all of them, to the warm apartment, to herself.

She meant it.

It occurred to her, standing there in the middle of everything she had tended and been tended by, that she had built a home. Not the walls-and-rent kind—she'd had that for three years—but the real kind. The kind made of presence and patience and the deliberate choice, every single morning, to keep showing up for the things that showed up for you.

Chapter 21: The First Warning Sign

It began with Fern.

One morning in the second week of January, Prachi was making her tea when she noticed it — a frond. Brown at the tip. Not the pale, hopeful browning of a new unfurling, but a deep, brittle, curling brown that spread inward from the edge like something retreating.

Prachi set her mug down slowly.

'Fern,' she said.

Fern did not respond. He had not rustled in two days, now that she thought about it. She had been busy with a new project and she had watered him on Tuesday and she thought — she had thought — everything was fine.

'Fern, look at me.'

He couldn't. He was a fern. But something about the stillness felt different. Heavier.

Prachi pulled out her phone and began searching with the frantic energy of someone who has just noticed a smoke alarm going off and doesn't know which room the fire is in.

Brown fern tips causes. Why is my fern dying. Can you save a browning fern. Overwatering vs underwatering fern how to tell.

The results were not comforting. Root rot. Fungal infection. Spider mites. Too much sun. Too little humidity. Wrong soil. Wrong pot. Wrong everything.

'Oh no,' she said. 'Oh no, oh no, oh no.'

She grabbed Fern's pot and examined his soil with two fingers thrust directly into it. Too wet? Too dry? She couldn't tell anymore. She pressed her face close to his fronds, peering for the tiny specks that would indicate a mite infestation, her eyes going wide.

Harvey hummed on steadily from across the room.

'Don't look at me like that,' Prachi snapped — and then realized she'd snapped at a heater, took a breath, and said, quietly, 'I'm sorry. I'm scared.'

Chapter 22: The Night Fern Got Worse

By Thursday, two more fronds had begun to brown. Prachi had moved Fern away from the window (too cold — possibly too cold), placed a humidity tray beneath him, started misting him twice a day, and ordered three different fungal treatments overnight delivery, only one of which she actually needed but she bought all three because decision-making under anxiety is not precision work.

She couldn't sleep on Thursday night. She sat next to Fern with her phone light, examining every frond.

'I don't know what I did wrong,' she told him. 'Tell me what I did wrong. You can tell me. I can take it.'

Fern was very still.

'Please don't die,' she said. Very quietly. Like a secret. 'You were the first one. You've been here since the beginning. You don't get to go anywhere.'

At 2 AM, she called her mother.

'Mumma,' she said, when the call connected and her mother's sleepy voice said hello. 'My fern is sick.'

There was a pause. Then, with the exact composure of a mother who has received stranger 2 AM calls: 'How sick?'

'Brown tips. Maybe root rot. I don't know. I can't tell.'

'Did you check the roots?'

'I — no. I was scared to unpot him.'

'Beta. You have to check the roots. If they're brown and mushy it's root rot, remove the damaged ones, repot in fresh dry soil. If they're white and firm he's probably just too dry in this winter air. Mist him.'

Prachi let out a long breath. 'Okay.'

'You're talking about the fern like it's a family member.'

'He is a family member.'

Her mother was quiet for exactly one second. 'Check the roots, Prachi. And go to sleep.'

Chapter 23: Harvey Fails

It happened on the coldest night Delhi had seen in eleven years.

Prachi was still in the middle of the fern crisis — she'd checked the roots (firm, white, one or two soft spots trimmed, repotted, recovering) — when she padded to the living room at midnight to find the room shockingly, brutally cold.

Harvey was silent.

She stared at him. He was dark. His coils were not glowing. He made no hum, no click, no warmth at all. He simply sat there, orange and inert, like a monument to warmth that could no longer deliver on its promise.

'Harvey,' she said. A whisper at first. Then louder. 'Harvey.'

Nothing.

The thermometer on the wall read seven degrees. Outside, it would be three, maybe two. The balcony door had a gap at the bottom and she could feel that cold now, pressing in under the door like something with intent.

She crouched in front of Harvey and checked his plug — still in. She checked the switch — still on. She pressed the reset button that she didn't know about until she looked it up frantically on her phone under: Bajaj room heater not turning on.

Nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

She sat on the cold floor in front of Harvey with a blanket around her shoulders and felt, absurdly, like she was sitting vigil. Like she was waiting for something to come back that she was not sure was coming back.

'This is too much,' she said. 'Fern gets sick and now you. This is excessive. This is cosmically excessive.' She pressed both palms flat against Harvey's cold metal casing. 'Come back,' she said. 'Please.'

Chapter 24: A Long Cold Night

She slept in her coat. Three blankets. A hot water bottle that went cold by 3 AM. She pulled Fern's pot close because plants release warmth and everything helps when you're cold and alone and slightly unhinged with worry at 3 in the morning.

Rosie, through the balcony door, was silent. Even she, with all her flair, seemed to understand that this was not a moment for performance.

The apartment had never felt so empty of warmth.

Prachi lay in the cold dark and thought: What would this place be without the things that hold it together? Not rhetorically. With real fear. Because she'd always thought of her apartment as a warm, alive, full-of-presence place — but right now Fern was fragile and Harvey was gone and the rose was outside in the cold and everything felt suddenly precarious. Like you'd pulled one piece from the bottom of a Jenga tower and now you could see how the whole thing had been balancing all along.

She fell asleep eventually. She always did. But the night felt long in the way that only anxious, cold, alone nights can feel — like every minute is three minutes and you are not sure morning is definitely coming.

At 6:17 AM, a click.

She felt it more than heard it. A soft mechanical click, like a tiny door opening somewhere. Then a faint warmth beginning at her ankles.

Prachi opened her eyes.

Harvey glowed. Low at first, like a pilot light finding itself after a long darkness. Then steadier. Then the familiar, steady, warm, there-he-is orange glow that filled the room in the grey winter dawn.

She burst into tears.

Of relief. Of exhaustion. Of the intense, almost embarrassing emotion of being kept warm by something that had found its way back to working.

'Harvey,' she said, her voice cracking. 'You absolute disaster. Don't ever do that again.'

Harvey hummed on, steady and warm and entirely himself. As if he had simply needed one very long night off, and now he was back, and he intended to stay.

Chapter 25: Rosie's Night of Terror

Four days later — when Fern was showing new growth at his tips and Harvey had been running perfectly since his mysterious one-night blackout — Prachi opened the balcony door in the morning and could not understand what she was seeing.

Rosie's petals were on the floor.

All of them. The entire enormous November bloom — every single petal — lying scattered across the balcony tiles like a crime scene. Rosie's stems were bare. She looked stripped and shocking in the pale morning light, like a stage with no curtain and no performance and no warning there would be none.

Prachi stood in the doorway for a full thirty seconds, unable to speak.

'Rosie,' she finally managed.

'Don't,' Rosie said. Her voice was different. Quieter. Stripped of its usual dramatic flourish in the way that Rosie herself was now stripped of petals — suddenly visible in a new, raw way.

'What happened?'

'The wind,' Rosie said. 'Last night. It was very cold and the wind came fast and I—' She stopped. 'I couldn't hold on. I tried to hold on, Prachi.'

Something about those words — I tried to hold on — made Prachi's chest compress.

She stepped fully onto the balcony, heedless of the cold, and sat down on the stool directly in front of Rosie's bare stems.

'Are you okay?' she asked. 'Are your roots okay? Are your canes—'

'I'm fine,' Rosie said. 'I always survive. You know that. I'm always fine. I'll bloom again in eight weeks.'

'That's not what I asked.'

Rosie was quiet.

'I asked if you're okay,' Prachi said softly. 'Not if you'll be okay. If you are okay right now.'

The pause that followed was longer than any Prachi had ever heard from Rosie.

'No,' Rosie said, very small. 'Right now I'm not. I lost everything I had. I know it grows back. I know it's just petals. But I had them, and now they're gone, and it feels—'

'Like starting over,' Prachi said.

'Yes.'

Prachi reached out both hands and cupped Rosie's barest cane — gently, carefully, like cupping a candle flame against wind. 'You've started over before,' she said. 'You'll start over again. And I will be here every single time an new bud shows up. Even at 2 AM. Even on a deadline. You know I always come.'

'I know you come,' Rosie said, barely above a whisper.

'Then let's start the count,' Prachi said. 'Eight weeks. Ready?'

A long silence. Then, gathering herself — gathering all that deep, inevitable, flamboyant Rosie-ness from somewhere below the bare canes: 'Ready.'

Chapter 26: After the Crisis

By the end of January, the three crises had resolved in the way that crises in small apartments usually do — not cleanly, not without marks, but resolvedly.

Fern had four new fronds unfurling. They were pale and tightly coiled, the way new things always begin — as questions — but they were definitively, unmistakably, there.

Harvey had not flickered once since his dark night. He ran at a steady 30% all day when Prachi was home, just enough warmth, exactly enough — the same as he had always been, before the fear set in.

Rosie was budding. Three small, tight buds on two separate canes. It would be eight weeks, as she'd said. Maybe nine. But they were coming. The evidence was already there if you looked closely enough.

Prachi sat on the rug one evening and looked around at all three of them and felt something she hadn't expected to feel: gratitude with a sharper edge. Not the soft, daily gratitude of routine and comfort — but the big, clear-eyed gratitude that comes only after a close call. After you've stood in a cold room at 3 AM and felt what the absence of warmth really means. After you've seen bare canes and been afraid of losing the bloom for good.

You didn't know what you had, the saying goes, until it was gone.

Prachi adjusted that: you didn't know how much it mattered until you almost lost it, and then you sat in the afterwards, when it came back, and the love was the same as it had always been but now it was held differently — more carefully, more consciously, more clearly.

'I'm not taking any of you for granted,' she said to the room. To all three of them. 'Not ever again.'

Fern uncurled one tiny new frond, pale as a secret.

Harvey glowed warm and steady.

A rosebud, outside on the balcony, tightened its spiral infinitesimally. Preparing. Gathering. Getting ready to be magnificent again.

January ticked toward February. The cold was not done. But neither were any of them.

She looked at Fern and thought: There is the part of me that is rooted and quiet and holds on.

She looked at Harvey and thought: There is the part of me that tries, every single day, to make the world warmer for the people in it.

She looked at Rosie—ridiculous, magnificent, impossible Rosie—and thought: There is the part of me that insists on beauty. That blooms on schedule regardless of everything. That refuses to disappear, even in winter. Even when I'm tired. Even when no one is looking.

Epilogue: What Prachi Knew

By February, Prachi had arrived at a conclusion. She had come to it slowly, through weeks of cold nights and early mornings and long conversations with beings who communicated in the language of warmth and growth and scent and persistence. It was not a complicated conclusion. It was, in fact, very simple:

She was not confused between Fern, Rosie, and Harvey. She was loved by all three, in the exact ways she needed to be loved, at the exact times she needed it. The confusion had never been about choosing. The confusion had been about believing she could have all of it—that she was allowed to be steadied and warmed and beautified all at once.

She was.

She repotted Fern in February, giving him more room to grow. She brought Rosie a new soil mix with added bone meal because she deserved extravagant nutrition. She dusted Harvey's coils and oiled his feet and made sure his timer was set correctly.

Then she sat in the middle of her living room, cross-legged on the rug, windows full of grey winter light, surrounded by the three of them, and she felt—undeniably, simply, completely—

At home.

'I love you,' she said, meaning all of it. The green. The warmth. The red. The hum. The held-ness of it all.

Fern unfurled one small new frond—pale green, curled like a question mark, the way all new things begin.

Harvey pulsed warmly, steady and sure and exactly what he had always been.

Rosie opened her newest bloom, the petals unfurling slowly in the winter light, red and alive and completely, characteristically excessive.

And Prachi Sharma, twenty-six years old, graphic designer, keeper of seventeen houseplants, one rose bush, and one steadfast room heater—sat in the center of all that love in the shapes it had found her, and smiled the kind of smile that reaches every room of a person.

The apartment smelled of rose and warm air and winter sunlight.

It smelled like home.

— The End —
